title: Q1 Quiz 1: Unreal Setup, Interface, Navigation, and Transforms

answers.correctAnswer: Blank
answers.explanation: The tutorial chooses Games > Blank so students begin with a clean project.

answers.correctAnswer: The class is starting with visual scripting instead of C++.
answers.explanation: The tutorial leaves the project on Blueprint because it is not focused on C++ code at this stage.

answers.correctAnswer: Outliner
answers.explanation: The Outliner lists objects in the current level and can be used to select or organize them.

answers.correctAnswer: Right mouse button
answers.explanation: Viewport WASD navigation works while the right mouse button is held down.

answers.correctAnswer: R
answers.explanation: W is move, E is rotate, and R is scale.

answers.correctAnswer: Content Drawer or Content Browser
answers.explanation: The Content Drawer/Browser is the asset and folder browser for the project.

answers.correctAnswer: F
answers.explanation: The F key focuses the viewport on the selected object.

answers.correctAnswer: Snapping
answers.explanation: Snapping constrains transforms to set increments such as centimeters or degrees.

answers.correctAnswer: Game View
answers.explanation: Game View is toggled with G and hides editor-only widgets.

answers.correctAnswer: Whether the right mouse button is being held
answers.explanation: The tutorial explains that WASD navigation requires holding the right mouse button.

answers.correctAnswer: The duplicate is in the same location as the original
answers.explanation: Ctrl+D creates the duplicate in the same spot, so the student needs to move the copy.

answers.correctAnswer: Window > Load Layout > Default Editor Layout
answers.explanation: The tutorial shows resetting the editor layout from the Window menu.

answers.correctAnswer: Choose Games > Blank; set project name/location; confirm Blueprint and Starter Content; click Create
answers.explanation: This follows the project creation workflow from the first Unreal setup lesson.

answers.correctAnswer: Add or duplicate an object; move it; rotate it; scale it; capture evidence
answers.explanation: Students should build or duplicate an object, transform it intentionally, and then document the result.

answers.correctAnswer: It stores and organizes project assets such as folders, maps, meshes, props, and starter content.
answers.explanation: The Content Drawer/Browser is the project asset area and is essential for finding and placing project content.

title: Q1 Quiz 2 Draft: Post-Process, Materials, and Texture Maps

answers.correctAnswer: It automatically adjusts rendered brightness as the camera views darker or brighter areas.
answers.explanation: Auto exposure changes perceived brightness, similar to eyes adapting when moving between dark and bright spaces.

answers.correctAnswer: To apply camera and image effects after the scene is rendered.
answers.explanation: Post-process settings affect the final rendered image, including exposure, bloom, vignette, and color changes.

answers.correctAnswer: To make the post-process settings affect the entire level.
answers.explanation: Without Infinite Extent Unbound, the effect only applies while the camera is inside the volume bounds.

answers.correctAnswer: Base Color
answers.explanation: Base Color is the material input for the main color information of a surface.

answers.correctAnswer: sRGB on for Base Color; sRGB off for Roughness and Normal maps.
answers.explanation: Base Color is color data, while Roughness and Normal maps store non-color information that should not be interpreted as color.

answers.correctAnswer: Bloom
answers.explanation: Bloom adds a visible glow around bright areas.

answers.correctAnswer: Vignette
answers.explanation: Vignette affects the edges of the frame and can change the mood or focus of an image.

answers.correctAnswer: PBR
answers.explanation: PBR stands for physically based rendering and uses realistic material values.

answers.correctAnswer: Normal map
answers.explanation: A normal map does not change the actual mesh shape, but it changes how light appears to hit the surface.

answers.correctAnswer: They should check that the exposure property checkbox is enabled, the volume affects the camera, and Infinite Extent Unbound is enabled if it needs to affect the whole level.
answers.explanation: Post-process values may not apply if the property is not enabled or if the camera is outside a bounded volume.

answers.correctAnswer: They likely forgot to press Apply and Save, or the material is not assigned to the object.
answers.explanation: Material changes must be applied and saved before they reliably update in the level.

answers.correctAnswer: They should check that sRGB is turned off for the roughness map.
answers.explanation: Roughness maps are data maps, so sRGB should be off.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Add a Post Process Volume. 2. Make the volume affect the intended area. 3. Enable the needed properties. 4. Adjust exposure or effects such as bloom, vignette, or saturation. 5. Capture comparison screenshots.
answers.explanation: Students should place and configure the volume before relying on the visual changes for evidence.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Import texture maps. 2. Check sRGB and compression settings. 3. Create a material. 4. Connect maps to the correct inputs. 5. Adjust tiling with Texture Coordinate or Multiply if needed. 6. Apply the material to a surface.
answers.explanation: Correct import settings should be checked before building and judging the final material, and tiling should be adjusted before evidence screenshots.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that a normal map fakes small surface bumps or direction changes so lighting looks more detailed without changing the mesh. It also explains that non-color data maps such as Roughness and Normal should have sRGB off because they store control data, not color.
answers.explanation: This checks conceptual understanding across normal maps and color-versus-data texture settings.

title: Q1 Quiz 3 Draft: Master Materials, Static Meshes, Migration, and Lumen

answers.correctAnswer: They let creators reuse one parent setup while changing exposed values in instances.
answers.explanation: A master material provides the reusable structure, while instances expose controlled settings for variation.

answers.correctAnswer: Scale, orientation, materials, and whether the asset appears correctly in the Content Drawer.
answers.explanation: Imported meshes should be verified for scale, placement, and missing material issues before evidence is submitted.

answers.correctAnswer: Migration includes dependencies such as materials and textures.
answers.explanation: Migration uses the asset report to copy related dependencies that the visible asset needs.

answers.correctAnswer: Light bouncing around a scene and filling areas indirectly.
answers.explanation: The transcript describes global illumination as light bounce that can also carry surface color into nearby areas.

answers.correctAnswer: Before and after screenshots from the same camera angle showing changed bounce light, shadow fill, or realism.
answers.explanation: Lighting comparison evidence is strongest when the camera angle stays controlled and the visual change is clear.

answers.correctAnswer: Master material or parent material
answers.explanation: A master or parent material holds the reusable graph that instances inherit from.

answers.correctAnswer: Dependency
answers.explanation: Dependencies can include materials, textures, meshes, or other linked project assets.

answers.correctAnswer: Static mesh
answers.explanation: A static mesh is a 3D model asset used for props, environment pieces, and other non-skeletal objects.

answers.correctAnswer: Real-time lighting or dynamic lighting
answers.explanation: The Lumen section focuses on dynamic global illumination that can respond as scene elements move.

answers.correctAnswer: They should check that values in the parent or master material were converted to named parameters and that Apply and Save were used.
answers.explanation: Material instances can only edit exposed parameters from the parent setup.

answers.correctAnswer: The student may have copied only the visible mesh or selected the wrong destination instead of using Migrate with dependencies into the Content folder.
answers.explanation: Missing materials often mean dependencies were not copied correctly or the destination workflow was wrong.

answers.correctAnswer: They should explain that exposure changes brightness, then test bounce light, indirect light, movable light behavior, or Lumen/global illumination evidence.
answers.explanation: Exposure and global illumination are related visually but are not the same concept.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Choose the asset or folder. 2. Choose Migrate. 3. Review the asset report. 4. Select the destination project Content folder. 5. Verify the migrated assets in the destination project.
answers.explanation: Students should use the migration workflow and verify the destination result before submitting evidence.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Frame a camera angle. 2. Capture the before screenshot. 3. Adjust the lighting or Lumen-related setting. 4. Capture the after screenshot from the same angle. 5. Write a reflection explaining the visual change.
answers.explanation: A controlled comparison makes lighting evidence clearer and easier to grade.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that reusable materials let artists create controlled variations without rebuilding graphs, while migration brings needed dependencies together so assets work in another project. Both reduce repeated work and prevent broken results.
answers.explanation: This checks cross-lesson understanding of reuse, dependencies, and efficient production workflow.

title: Q1 Quiz 4 Draft: Lighting, Landscapes, Foliage, and Mini-Worlds

answers.correctAnswer: Point light
answers.explanation: A point light acts like a bulb and emits light outward in all directions.

answers.correctAnswer: Directional light
answers.explanation: Directional lights are used for sun-like light from an extremely far distance.

answers.correctAnswer: To soften harsh terrain bumps or sharp artifacts.
answers.explanation: Smooth reduces harsh changes in the landscape heightmap.

answers.correctAnswer: Too much foliage can hide the path, hurt readability, or affect performance.
answers.explanation: The transcript shows density being reduced repeatedly so trees and foliage do not overwhelm the scene.

answers.correctAnswer: How a scene could become interactive or playable.
answers.explanation: The Blueprint preview connects the environment to simple player interaction without requiring a full game system.

answers.correctAnswer: Spotlight
answers.explanation: Spotlights use cone angles to control the beam and falloff.

answers.correctAnswer: Landscape
answers.explanation: The Landscape system is used to create and edit terrain.

answers.correctAnswer: Player path
answers.explanation: A player path helps make a mini-world readable and playable.

answers.correctAnswer: Blueprint
answers.explanation: Blueprints use nodes and visual scripting to create behavior without writing traditional code.

answers.correctAnswer: They should adjust lighting direction, intensity, color, shadow, or placement to create a clear focal point.
answers.explanation: Lighting should guide attention, not just make the whole scene equally bright.

answers.correctAnswer: They should use Smooth to soften harsh shapes and adjust brush size or strength for more controlled sculpting.
answers.explanation: The transcript shows Smooth as the fix for harsh landscapes after aggressive sculpting.

answers.correctAnswer: They should check Align to Normal and turn it off if trees should grow straight up.
answers.explanation: The transcript specifically notes unchecking Align to Normal so trees do not tilt with the landscape slope.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Create the landscape. 2. Sculpt broad forms. 3. Flatten a usable area. 4. Smooth harsh edges. 5. Capture evidence screenshots.
answers.explanation: Landscape work should move from broad terrain creation into refinement and evidence capture.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Create terrain. 2. Add the main path or focal point. 3. Paint foliage. 4. Adjust density and scale. 5. Play or camera test. 6. Submit evidence.
answers.explanation: The mini-world process should keep readability and player experience ahead of random decoration.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that lighting creates mood and focus, terrain creates readable forms or paths, and foliage/props add believable detail without hiding the design. It should mention evidence such as screenshots, path readability, or focal point.
answers.explanation: This checks synthesis across scene lighting, landscape shaping, and mini-world finishing.

title: Q3 Quiz 1 Draft: Production, Documentation, Planning, Setup, and Blockout

answers.correctAnswer: To track what was built, captured, changed, and planned next.
answers.explanation: A production log records work and decisions so the documentary has accurate process evidence.

answers.correctAnswer: A short screen recording showing a decision being made.
answers.explanation: Process evidence shows how the project changed, not just the final state.

answers.correctAnswer: It helps the viewer know what to notice first.
answers.explanation: A focal point gives the environment visual priority and direction.

answers.correctAnswer: It shows whether doors, paths, walls, and spaces feel believable from player view.
answers.explanation: The player character helps test whether the environment feels readable and walkable.

answers.correctAnswer: B-roll
answers.explanation: B-roll supports the main story with useful detail or process footage.

answers.correctAnswer: Production log
answers.explanation: The production log protects the process story from being forgotten.

answers.correctAnswer: They should record the work process early, capture before/after evidence, and update the production log while they build.
answers.explanation: The documentary needs daily process evidence, not only final screenshots.

answers.correctAnswer: They should test from player view and adjust scale, doors, paths, walls, or camera framing based on the character size.
answers.explanation: Player-view testing reveals scale problems that editor view can hide.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Build or revise the castle. 2. Record screen/camera evidence. 3. Update the production log. 4. Upload evidence to Drive or YouTube. 5. Submit the link in DCC Creative Studio.
answers.explanation: This sequence keeps production work and evidence connected.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that daily recording captures the process, decisions, mistakes, revisions, and progress that a final screenshot cannot show. It should connect the evidence to the final making-of documentary.
answers.explanation: This checks whether students understand the documentary purpose of daily evidence.

title: Q3 Quiz 2 Draft: Materials, Terrain Assets, Modular Kits, Composition, and Process Evidence

answers.correctAnswer: To make terrain zones readable and believable.
answers.explanation: Materials help viewers understand paths, cliffs, grass, and other terrain surfaces.

answers.correctAnswer: It matches scale, style, and story purpose.
answers.explanation: Imported assets should serve the environment rather than replace design decisions.

answers.correctAnswer: They allow repeated pieces to create larger structures efficiently.
answers.explanation: Modular kits make construction faster while still requiring variation and planning.

answers.correctAnswer: The overall readable shape of the castle from a distance.
answers.explanation: A strong silhouette helps the castle read clearly before small details are visible.

answers.correctAnswer: Modular kit
answers.explanation: A modular kit contains pieces that can be combined into larger readable forms.

answers.correctAnswer: Silhouette
answers.explanation: Silhouette is the readable outer shape that helps viewers identify the castle.

answers.correctAnswer: They should choose fewer assets, check scale/style, place them with purpose, and document why each supports the castle environment.
answers.explanation: Asset use should support design rather than create random clutter.

answers.correctAnswer: The student can vary height, spacing, rotation, damage/detail pieces, surrounding props, or camera composition while keeping the structure organized.
answers.explanation: Variation makes modular construction feel more believable.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Review kit pieces. 2. Block the castle silhouette. 3. Assemble modular pieces. 4. Add variation. 5. Record progress evidence.
answers.explanation: The castle should move from broad readable form to more varied detail.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that each choice shows how the castle changed over time and why the environment became more readable, believable, or story-driven. It should mention screenshots, recordings, or production log evidence.
answers.explanation: This checks whether students connect production choices to documentary evidence.

title: Q3 Quiz 3 Draft: Framing, Foliage, Storytelling, Lighting Mood, and Documentary Capture

answers.correctAnswer: Guide the viewer toward important areas.
answers.explanation: Framing uses foreground, middle ground, and background to direct attention.

answers.correctAnswer: When it hides paths, focal points, or important silhouettes.
answers.explanation: Too much foliage can reduce readability or create performance concerns.

answers.correctAnswer: Using visual details to imply history, purpose, or events.
answers.explanation: Environmental storytelling lets viewers infer story from the scene itself.

answers.correctAnswer: Adjusting lighting choices to support a clear emotional tone.
answers.explanation: Lighting mood uses color, direction, contrast, and intensity to shape feeling.

answers.correctAnswer: Story details or environmental storytelling details
answers.explanation: Story details give the environment meaning beyond decoration.

answers.correctAnswer: Foreground
answers.explanation: Foreground elements can guide attention and add depth.

answers.correctAnswer: They should reduce density, clear the path, group foliage intentionally, and record before/after readability evidence.
answers.explanation: Foliage should support the route and mood, not hide navigation.

answers.correctAnswer: They should choose one story idea, remove random details, and arrange a few visible clues that support that idea.
answers.explanation: Storytelling works best when details point to a clear interpretation.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Choose a mood word. 2. Capture before evidence. 3. Adjust lighting. 4. Capture after evidence. 5. Explain the change.
answers.explanation: Lighting mood evidence is strongest when before and after are comparable.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer explains that these choices help viewers understand where to look, what changed, what the castle feels like, and what story the build communicates. It should mention visual evidence or production clips.
answers.explanation: This checks synthesis across visual storytelling and documentation.

title: Q3 Quiz 4 Draft: Post-Process, Walkthroughs, Documentary Evidence, and Portfolio Submission

answers.correctAnswer: To present the scene with intentional framing, mood, and clarity.
answers.explanation: A cinematic screenshot should look purposeful and readable.

answers.correctAnswer: It can polish mood and image quality without hiding readability.
answers.explanation: Post-process supports the final look but should not obscure the scene.

answers.correctAnswer: Pacing, route readability, collision issues, and how the scene feels in motion.
answers.explanation: Walkthroughs show the experience over time.

answers.correctAnswer: How the castle was made and improved over time.
answers.explanation: The documentary should tell the process story, not only show the final product.

answers.correctAnswer: Walkthrough
answers.explanation: A walkthrough shows how the environment feels while moving through it.

answers.correctAnswer: Cinematic screenshot or portfolio screenshot
answers.explanation: A cinematic or portfolio screenshot presents the project intentionally.

answers.correctAnswer: They should plan a slower route, pause at focal points, re-record from player view, and check that viewers can understand the scene.
answers.explanation: Walkthrough pacing should help viewers read the environment.

answers.correctAnswer: The sharing permissions are likely restricted; the student should set Drive or YouTube sharing so the teacher can view the evidence before submitting.
answers.explanation: Link evidence only works when sharing permissions are correct.

answers.correctAnswer: 1. Select strongest evidence. 2. Organize the folder or links. 3. Check sharing permissions. 4. Write the final reflection. 5. Submit the link in DCC Creative Studio.
answers.explanation: Final submission needs organization and accessible links.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer names early, middle, and final process evidence such as screen recordings, camera clips, screenshots, walkthroughs, production log entries, and reflection. It explains that the documentary should show how decisions and revisions created the final castle.
answers.explanation: This checks final synthesis of the castle documentary process.

title: Q2 Quiz 1 Draft: Resolve Setup, Media, Timelines, and Rough Cuts

answers.correctAnswer: Creating, opening, backing up, and organizing Resolve projects.
answers.explanation: The Project Manager is where Resolve projects are created, opened, exported, and organized.

answers.correctAnswer: Media
answers.explanation: The Media page is designed for finding, importing, previewing, and organizing clips.

answers.correctAnswer: Clips, bins, timelines, and other media that live in the project.
answers.explanation: The Media Pool is the project area that contains imported media and project assets.

answers.correctAnswer: They organize clips into meaningful project folders.
answers.explanation: Bins keep footage grouped so editors can find clips faster.

answers.correctAnswer: Matching separate audio and video so sound lines up correctly.
answers.explanation: Audio sync aligns separately recorded sound with video, often using waveforms.

answers.correctAnswer: A clip before it is edited into the timeline.
answers.explanation: The source viewer previews source media before it becomes part of the edit.

answers.correctAnswer: The edited sequence at the current playhead position.
answers.explanation: The timeline viewer shows what the active edit looks like at the playhead.

answers.correctAnswer: The selected start of the usable part of a source clip.
answers.explanation: An in point marks where the selected range begins.

answers.correctAnswer: The selected end of the usable part of a source clip.
answers.explanation: An out point marks where the selected range ends.

answers.correctAnswer: An early edit focused on order, timing, and story.
answers.explanation: A rough cut gets the story working before polish passes.

answers.correctAnswer: Removing or restoring clip edges to control timing.
answers.explanation: Trimming adjusts where a clip starts or ends in the edit.

answers.correctAnswer: Unwanted gaps after shortening or extending edits.
answers.explanation: Ripple edits move timeline material so timing stays connected.

answers.correctAnswer: Track
answers.explanation: Tracks organize timeline media into video or audio lanes.

answers.correctAnswer: Metadata
answers.explanation: Metadata helps editors identify and filter clips.

answers.correctAnswer: Organizing footage makes clips easier to find, reduces confusion, and speeds up editing decisions.
answers.explanation: A strong answer connects organization to faster, clearer editing.

title: Q2 Quiz 2 Draft: Titles, Transitions, and Final Export

answers.correctAnswer: Readable text such as a title card, name label, or lower third.
answers.explanation: Titles communicate text information inside the video.

answers.correctAnswer: Inspector
answers.explanation: The Inspector exposes controls for selected clips, titles, transitions, and effects.

answers.correctAnswer: A point in time that stores a value so a property can change over time.
answers.explanation: Keyframes make motion or property changes happen over time.

answers.correctAnswer: When it supports the edit and does not distract from the story.
answers.explanation: Transitions should serve clarity, pacing, or tone instead of becoming decoration.

answers.correctAnswer: It is readable, spelled correctly, timed well, and supports the viewer.
answers.explanation: Student titles should improve communication without hiding the video.

answers.correctAnswer: Deliver
answers.explanation: The Deliver page is used to render the timeline into a finished video file.

answers.correctAnswer: A list of export jobs waiting to render.
answers.explanation: Resolve adds export jobs to the render queue before rendering.

answers.correctAnswer: So the exported file can be found and submitted correctly.
answers.explanation: Naming and location checks prevent lost exports and confusing submissions.

answers.correctAnswer: A method used to encode and compress video or audio data.
answers.explanation: Codecs such as H.264 and H.265 affect how a video file is encoded.

answers.correctAnswer: H.264 in an MP4 container.
answers.explanation: H.264 MP4 files are widely playable and practical for class review.

answers.correctAnswer: To catch gaps, unreadable titles, timing problems, or other mistakes.
answers.explanation: A final playback check catches issues before a file is submitted.

answers.correctAnswer: Guide attention or polish the communication without distracting.
answers.explanation: Motion should be intentional and support the edit.

answers.correctAnswer: Transition
answers.explanation: Transitions control how one clip changes into another.

answers.correctAnswer: Render queue
answers.explanation: The render queue stores export jobs before rendering begins.

answers.correctAnswer: A strong answer can include title readability, spelling, timing, timeline gaps, final playback, file name, save location, or export settings.
answers.explanation: This checks whether students can name real pre-export checks that affect the viewer or submission workflow.
